FT_IC1.evidence: Explicitly states data from ABCD Study, N=10,246, baseline 2016-2020, ages 9-14

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures both total screen time (six modalities: TV, YouTube videos, video games, texting, video chatting, social media) and problematic screen use (Social Media Addiction Questionnaire [SMAQ] and Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire [MPIQ]). Specific phone/SM variables separately extractable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses linking screen time/problematic screen use to eating disorder symptoms (fear of obesity, self-worth tied to weight, compensatory behaviors, binge eating, distress). Table 2: OR 1.05-1.55 for total screen time; Table 3: OR 1.26-1.82 for problematic use.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric values reported: Table 2 shows ORs (1.05-1.55) with 95% CIs and p-values (<0.001 to 0.025); Table 3 shows ORs (1.26-1.82) with 95% CIs and p-values (<0.001 to 0.004)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Prospective cohort design (baseline screen time → 2-year outcome) plus cross-sectional analyses at year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, Eating and Weight Disorders, September 2024. Not a preprint or deposit.

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Paper includes cross-sectional analyses (year 2 problematic screen use as predictor in same year). Paper also reports phone/SM as IV predicting non-SM outcomes (eating disorder symptoms). Neither condition for FT-EC7 met.
notes: Clear INCLUDE. Multiple phone/SM variables with separate coefficients reported. Longitudinal design with numeric coefficients for screen exposure predicting eating symptoms.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, N=8,489 from longitudinal study in United States

FT_IC2.evidence: No specific smartphone or social media variable measured. Paper examines pet ownership impact on family involvement in sports/activities, physical activity, and screen time. Screen time mentioned only as generic outcome (result: 'no meaningful relationship'), not as specific phone/SM exposure.

FT_IC3.evidence: Cannot determine from available text whether inferential tests link phone/SM variables to non-SM outcomes, since FT-IC2 is not met

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis using multiple logistic and linear regressions

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable. Paper does not measure specific smartphone/SM variable, so directionality criteria do not apply. Fails FT-IC2.
notes: EXCLUDE. Does not meet FT-IC2. Screen time measured as generic outcome (no phone/SM specificity). Paper focuses on pet ownership as predictor of activity engagement; screen time is incidental outcome variable.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data, N=11,876 adolescents assessed annually from ages 9-12

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen usage types: TV, video games, online video, social media, texting, video chat. Each modality assessed separately at baseline. Specific phone/SM variables include social media, texting, and video chat.

FT_IC3.evidence: Longitudinal network analysis examining associations between screen usage types and psychotic-like experiences (PLEs) across three time points. Network analysis tests connectivity between screen usage and PLE symptoms (hallucinations, concentration difficulties, etc.)

FT_IC4.evidence: Tables 2-4 report edge strengths (numeric values) across the network at different time points. Network analysis with centrality measures, betweenness, closeness, and expected influence reported with numeric values (e.g., Table 2: edge strengths 0.164-0.362; Table 3: 0.001-0.445; Table 4: 0.004-0.469)

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical ABCD analysis. Longitudinal network models examining screen usage and PLE associations across three time points.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article, Comprehensive Psychiatry, June 2024

FT_EC7_reason: Does not apply. Paper reports screen usage (TV, video games, online video, social media, texting, video chat) as predictor of PLEs (non-SM outcomes: hallucinations, concentration difficulties). Screen time is IV, PLEs are DV. Meets FT-IC3 as screen→PLE association.
notes: Clear INCLUDE. Network analysis with specific screen modalities (including social media, texting) predicting psychotic-like experiences. Numeric edge strengths reported across multiple time points.
